customSentences.text: Small changes tend to add up over time, even when you can't see them straight away.
I've been trying to get into the habit of going to bed at the same time every night.
We tend to overestimate what we can do in a day and underestimate what we can do in a year.

glossary.meaning: judge as more / less than something really is

customSentences.text: It's not really about setting bigger goals — it's more about building better systems.
Every action you take is a kind of vote for the person you're becoming.
The trick is to make the new habit so easy that you almost can't say no.

glossary.meaning: the key / the clever part is

customSentences.text: Once it becomes part of your routine, you barely have to think about it.
Most people give up just before they would have seen real progress.
Your environment shapes your behaviour more than you'd probably like to admit.
If you want to change what you do, you might need to change who you think you are.
Two minutes a day is often enough to get the ball rolling.
Honestly, the hardest part is usually just getting started.
You don't really rise to the level of your goals — you fall to the level of your systems.

glossary.word: rise / fall to the level of
glossary.meaning: perform up to / down to a standard

customSentences.text: It might be worth stacking a new habit on top of one you already have.

glossary.meaning: add / pile on (here: chain habits together)

customSentences.text: It's worth keeping track of your progress, even if it feels a bit silly at first.
I'd rather make a tiny bit of progress every day than burn out trying to do too much at once.
When you keep showing up, the results tend to take care of themselves.
It's been said that you become the average of the five people you spend the most time with.
I came across this idea a few years ago and it really stuck with me.
There isn't really one big decision that changes your life — it's the cumulative effect that matters.
If you can't seem to stick with a habit, the friction is probably too high.
Just because you fell off doesn't mean you have to start over from scratch.
Missing once might be an accident, but missing twice could be the start of a new habit.
Don't break the chain — and if you do, try not to break it twice in a row.

glossary.meaning: interrupt a streak / give up the run

customSentences.text: It turns out that motivation is overrated; consistency is what actually moves the needle.

glossary.meaning: given more value than it deserves

customSentences.text: Bad habits tend to creep in when you're not paying attention.
The cue, the craving, the response, and the reward — that's the loop you've got to be aware of.
I'd say the key is to make it obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
Once you've identified the trigger, you can start to interrupt the pattern.
I'm a firm believer that environment beats willpower nine times out of ten.
The thing about discipline is that it tends to get easier the more you practise it.
Identity-based habits stick because they're tied to who you are, not just what you want.
I don't think anyone's really got it all figured out — we're all just trying to get a little better.
Going from zero to one is usually harder than going from one to ten.
Sometimes you just have to show up, even when you really don't feel like it.
Letting yourself off the hook one time can quickly become a slippery slope.

glossary.meaning: excuse yourself / not hold yourself accountable
course of action leading to bad outcomes

customSentences.text: I've come to realise that small wins matter more than big breakthroughs.
It might seem trivial, but those tiny improvements really do compound over time.
Once you've built up some momentum, it's easier to keep going than to stop.
If I were you, I'd start with whatever feels almost too easy to skip.
I'm going to give him the benefit of the doubt for now.

glossary.word: give someone the benefit of the doubt
glossary.meaning: choose to believe someone is honest/right when you're not 100% sure

customSentences.text: To be fair, she hasn't really had a chance to explain herself yet.
I wouldn't read too much into it if I were you.

glossary.meaning: find meaning that isn't really there

customSentences.text: That's not what I meant — let me put it differently.
I have a feeling something's off, but I can't quite explain why.
We've been going back and forth on this for weeks now.
I'd hate to put you on the spot, but what's your take?
To cut a long story short, we ended up cancelling the trip.

glossary.word: to cut a long story short
glossary.meaning: to summarise briefly / skip the details

customSentences.text: It looks like we'll have to make do with what we've got.
I've been keeping an eye on the situation, just in case.
Now that you mention it, I do remember something about that.
I don't want to jump the gun, but it's looking promising.

glossary.meaning: act too soon / before the right time

customSentences.text: Let's not get ahead of ourselves — there's still a lot to do.
I'm willing to give it a shot if you are.
To be honest, I'm not sure that's a hill worth dying on.

glossary.meaning: an issue important enough to fight hard for

customSentences.text: I'd rather not get involved in this if I can help it.
Could we possibly push the meeting back a bit?
I'd hate to leave you hanging, so let me know either way.

glossary.meaning: fail to give a needed response / keep someone waiting

customSentences.text: You'd think they would have figured it out by now.

glossary.meaning: one would expect (used with mild frustration)

customSentences.text: I'll admit, I didn't see that one coming.
Looking on the bright side, at least we learned something.
I'm trying to figure out where things went wrong.
The way I see it, we have two reasonable options.
It's a tough call, but I'd lean towards the safer one.
Sometimes you've just got to roll with the punches.

glossary.meaning: adapt to whatever happens / accept difficulties calmly

customSentences.text: I don't want to come off as ungrateful, but I expected more.

glossary.meaning: appear to be / give the impression of

customSentences.text: That's water under the bridge as far as I'm concerned.

glossary.meaning: a past problem that doesn't matter anymore

customSentences.text: I wouldn't lose any sleep over it if I were you.
They've been giving me the runaround for months.

glossary.meaning: deliberately delay or avoid giving a clear answer

customSentences.text: To put it bluntly, we can't afford another mistake.

glossary.meaning: to say it directly / without softening

customSentences.text: I'm in no rush — take your time.
Off the top of my head, I'd say around fifty.

glossary.word: off the top of my head
glossary.meaning: without thinking carefully / as an estimate

customSentences.text: I'd say it's worth giving them a second chance.
It crossed my mind, but I dismissed the idea pretty quickly.
I'm not going to pretend I understand everything yet.
Don't get me wrong — I'm not blaming you.
I might be reading between the lines, but it sounds personal.

glossary.meaning: understand a hidden meaning / what isn't said directly

customSentences.text: It depends on how strict they want to be about it.
I'd take that with a pinch of salt if I were you.

glossary.word: take it with a pinch of salt
glossary.meaning: don't fully believe it / be sceptical

customSentences.text: We should really nip this in the bud before it gets worse.

glossary.meaning: stop it early before it grows into a problem

customSentences.text: There's a thin line between being honest and being rude.

glossary.meaning: a small difference separating two things

customSentences.text: I'll be the first to admit I was wrong about that.
Let's not beat around the bush — what do you actually want?

glossary.meaning: avoid the main point / talk indirectly

customSentences.text: He's been bending over backwards to make things right.

glossary.meaning: make a great effort to help / please someone

customSentences.text: You can't please everyone, no matter how hard you try.
I get where you're coming from, but I still have my doubts.
I'd take a rain check, if that's alright with you.

glossary.meaning: politely postpone an invitation to another time

customSentences.text: Honestly, that sounds like more trouble than it's worth.

glossary.meaning: not worth the effort it requires

customSentences.text: We're not seeing eye to eye on this, are we?
Let's call it a day and pick it up tomorrow.
Thanks for having me — I'd like to walk you through the architecture work we've done for Kapalsantai.
Just to give you some context, Kapalsantai is an online boat tour marketplace based in Indonesia.
Before we dive into the details, let me quickly set the scene.
The platform grew pretty organically, which means a lot of decisions were never written down.

glossary.meaning: developed gradually without a master plan

customSentences.text: What we set out to do was take a step back and reconstruct the architecture deliberately.

glossary.meaning: pause and look at the bigger picture

customSentences.text: We used the arc42 framework because it gave us a structured way to capture both the decisions and the trade-offs.
At a high level, we connect international tourists with local boat operators in the Labuan Bajo region.
The honest reality is, technical debt had been piling up silently, and nobody had a clear picture of why.
So with that in mind, let's look at where we are today.

glossary.meaning: considering that / keeping that in view

customSentences.text: Moving on, I'd like to spend a few minutes on the quality attributes.
Now, this is where it gets interesting.
I'll come back to that point in a moment — bear with me.
Let me pause there for a second and unpack what this actually means.
If we zoom out a bit, the bigger picture looks like this.
I'll skim over the details here, but the key takeaway is on the next slide.

glossary.meaning: cover quickly / not go into depth

customSentences.text: Let's shift gears and talk about the proposed solution.
Before I move on, are we all on the same page so far?
Out of the nine ISO 25010 categories, we deliberately picked just two as Priority 1.
The reasoning behind that is simple — you can't optimise for everything at once.

glossary.meaning: try to make everything equally good

customSentences.text: Security ended up being our top driver, particularly around payment data.
Performance Efficiency came in a close second, mainly because of the search experience.
Think of the quality tree as a way of breaking down what 'good' actually looks like.
An ADR — or Architecture Decision Record — is basically a one-pager that captures why we made a particular call.
What we're really trying to do here is make the trade-offs visible.
In plain English, if it's not written down, it didn't happen.
We've quantified each scenario using a weighted score, so the priorities aren't just opinions.
Confidentiality and Authenticity are the two sub-attributes we picked under Security.
We had to weigh up speed of delivery against long-term maintainability.
There's a genuine trade-off here between flexibility and operational complexity.
Looking back, we'd probably do it differently — but at the time, it made sense.
If you ask me, this was the most important call we made during the whole process.
We deliberately chose to keep things simple, even though it meant cutting some corners.
The alternative would have been more elegant, but it just wasn't worth the overhead.
Frankly, this is the kind of decision you can only second-guess in hindsight.

glossary.meaning: looking back / with the benefit of later knowledge

customSentences.text: We picked Next.js over Angular mainly for developer productivity reasons.
Going with a managed database saved us a lot of headaches around backups and patching.
It's a balancing act between getting things shipped and keeping the system clean.
Perahuku is our dedicated provider portal — it's where boat operators manage their day-to-day.
Right now, providers update their availability manually, and that's where most of the friction lives.
Instant booking is what we're moving towards, but provider adoption isn't quite there yet.
We're trying to bridge the gap between how customers want to book and how providers actually operate.
There's a real usability issue with the calendar — operators just aren't using it the way we'd hoped.
For now, we're treating Perahuku as an internal system under study rather than baking it deeply into the architecture.

glossary.meaning: integrate as a fundamental part of

customSentences.text: The booking and payment object lifecycles are really at the heart of what we're modelling.
We're going to dig deeper into the payment flow because that's where the regulatory risk sits.
The current state, or IST, is what's running in production today.
The proposed state, the SOLL, is what we're aiming towards over the next few quarters.
I'll be honest — there's still a fair bit we haven't pinned down.
This part is more of a work in progress than a finished design.
We're aware this isn't perfect, but it's the best we've got given the budget constraints.
Hand on heart, this is the area where we're least confident.
Availability is something we know is going to bite us eventually, but it's not a priority today.
That's a fair question — let me think about it for a second.
Honestly, I don't have a definitive answer to that right now.
Could you walk me through what you're trying to get at?
Let me come back to that one after I've had a chance to check the details.
I'd rather get back to you with a proper answer than make something up on the spot.
Just to make sure I've understood you correctly — are you asking about the payment side or the booking side?
To wrap things up, the main takeaway is this: architecture isn't a one-time effort, it's an ongoing conversation.
If there's one thing I'd like you to remember, it's that making the trade-offs visible is half the battle.

glossary.meaning: a major part of the difficulty

customSentences.text: Thanks for sticking with me — I'm happy to take any questions you've got.

glossary.meaning: stay attentive / be patient with me

customSentences.text: The context view essentially shows who the system talks to and what flows in and out.
The building block view is where we break the system down into its main components.
We've kept the runtime view focused on the happy path — error handling branches off separately.

glossary.meaning: the normal flow when everything works

customSentences.text: On the deployment view, you can see what runs where and how it's all wired up.
We deliberately kept the diagrams at a fairly abstract level so they don't go out of date overnight.
If you want the full detail, that's all in the appendix — feel free to dig in afterwards.
What I'm showing you here is the SOLL state — the IST is in the backup slides.

glossary.meaning: extra slides for reference, not in the main flow

customSentences.text: The boundaries of the system are drawn around what we actively control.
Anything sitting outside that boundary, we treat as a black box.

glossary.meaning: something whose internals we don't see/care about

customSentences.text: I want to be upfront that some of the diagrams are still evolving as we get more feedback.
We used DDD selectively, mostly at the level where booking and payment interact.
Bounded contexts gave us a way to draw clear lines between different parts of the domain.
Within a bounded context, the team can speak its own language without stepping on anyone's toes.
Booking and payment are two separate aggregates, each with its own lifecycle.

glossary.meaning: a cluster of related domain objects treated as one unit (DDD)

customSentences.text: We avoid sharing state directly between aggregates — communication goes through events.
The ubiquitous language is something we've tried to nail down with the operators themselves.
There's no point inventing fancy terminology if the business doesn't recognise it.
A booking goes through several states — pending, confirmed, paid, completed, refunded.
State-based management lets us reason clearly about what's allowed at each step.
If you're not careful with aggregates, you end up with a distributed monolith.
We picked PostgreSQL because it gave us the most flexibility without locking us into a vendor.

glossary.meaning: tie you to / make you dependent on

customSentences.text: Going with a managed database lets us sleep at night.
Backups, patching, failover — all of that is handled for us, which frees up the team.
We're not at a scale where sharding makes sense yet.
Schema changes go through migrations — we don't touch production directly.

glossary.meaning: modify live systems without proper process

customSentences.text: JSON columns have been a lifesaver for some of the more flexible fields.

glossary.meaning: extremely helpful / something that solves a problem

customSentences.text: We're using soft deletes everywhere — nothing actually gets removed from the database.

glossary.meaning: marking a record as deleted without removing it

customSentences.text: If we ever outgrow the current setup, we've left room to split things out later.
We've stuck with REST for now because everyone on the team is comfortable with it.
GraphQL was tempting, but the added complexity wasn't going to pay off at our scale.
Every endpoint is versioned, so we can evolve the API without breaking existing clients.
Authentication is handled by JWT tokens with a short expiry.
We try to keep the API surface as small as possible — fewer endpoints means fewer attack vectors.
OpenAPI specs are the source of truth — the code generates from them.
Rate limiting is in place to keep the system honest under load.

glossary.meaning: keep within its limits / prevent misuse

customSentences.text: Idempotency keys make sure that retrying a payment request doesn't double-charge anyone.

glossary.meaning: property that repeating an operation gives the same result

customSentences.text: We went with Next.js because server-side rendering matters for SEO on a travel site.
Vue was on the shortlist, but the team had more hands-on experience with React.
Angular felt like overkill for what we actually need.
We render the listing pages on the server so tourists don't see a blank screen first.

glossary.meaning: empty page (typical of slow client-side apps)

customSentences.text: The booking flow is mostly client-side because it's highly interactive.
We don't ship any framework that we can't justify with a concrete need.
Performance budgets are something we actually enforce, not just talk about.
Mobile is where most of our traffic comes from, so we design for that first.
The booking process is the spine of the platform — everything else hangs off it.
On this BPMN diagram, you can see the happy path going straight across.
Anything that's not the happy path branches off — that keeps the diagram readable.
Payment confirmation is the trickiest part — there are several edge cases that need handling.

glossary.meaning: an unusual situation at the boundary of normal behaviour

customSentences.text: Provider confirmation used to be unstructured, but the new flow makes it explicit.
We're moving from a manual back-and-forth to an instant-booking model.
If we can pull that off, the customer experience improves dramatically.
The payment flow has a built-in reimbursement path for when a booking falls through.
Cross-cutting concerns are the things that touch everything — logging, monitoring, security.
We've kept these consolidated rather than spreading them across each module.
Structured logging means we can actually search through what happened, not just scroll endlessly.

glossary.meaning: look through huge amounts of text without finding anything

customSentences.text: Observability is something we under-invested in early on and have been catching up on since.

glossary.meaning: make up for lost time on / fix later

customSentences.text: Tracing lets us follow a single request all the way through the system.
Without good metrics, you're flying blind when something starts to slow down.
Health checks are wired into the deployment pipeline — if something fails, the rollout stops.

glossary.meaning: the process of releasing a new version

customSentences.text: We don't have a fancy SIEM, but we do log every authentication event.

glossary.meaning: Security Information and Event Management — security monitoring system

customSentences.text: Everything ships through CI — there's no manual deployment anymore.
We do blue-green deployments to keep downtime to a minimum.
Rolling back is a one-click operation — that's saved us more than once.
Infrastructure is defined as code, so anyone on the team can spin up an environment.
We host on a managed cloud — the cost story works out cleaner that way.
Secrets live in a dedicated vault, never in the codebase.
Feature flags let us roll out changes gradually and pull them back if something breaks.
Staging mirrors production as closely as we can afford.
From a business standpoint, the key win here is faster booking turnaround.

glossary.meaning: looking at it from the business perspective

customSentences.text: What this means in practice is operators spend less time chasing confirmations.
The technical side is one thing, but the impact on day-to-day operations is what really matters.
If we get this right, we should see the conversion rate move noticeably.
There's a clear ROI on cleaning up the booking flow, even before we get to the architecture benefits.
Stakeholder buy-in is half the work — the other half is building the thing.
We've been clear with the team about what's in scope and what's not.
I don't want to oversell this — there will be plenty of work after the architecture is in place.
The roadmap stays the same — what changes is how confidently we can deliver it.
At the end of the day, our job is to make sure the system can grow with the business.

glossary.word: at the end of the day
glossary.meaning: ultimately / when all is said and done

customSentences.text: We've made a deliberate distinction between risks, non-risks, and sensitivity points.
A trade-off is a known compromise — a risk is an unknown that could bite us.
Documenting risks isn't about being negative — it's about being prepared.

glossary.meaning: be ready for what might come

customSentences.text: For each risk, we've tried to capture a realistic mitigation plan.
Some risks we're choosing to accept rather than mitigate — that's a legitimate decision too.
The biggest risk we see is around third-party dependencies that we don't control.
Sendgrid and WhatsApp are essentially constraints we have to live with.
Budget itself is a constraint, not a risk — we know it, we plan around it.
If I had to summarise this in one sentence, it would be: making decisions explicit.
Bear in mind that what we're showing here is a snapshot — the system keeps evolving.

glossary.meaning: the state at a particular moment

customSentences.text: I won't go through every single decision, but I'm happy to dig into any of them in Q&A.
Just before I move on, let me highlight one thing that often gets overlooked.
On a related note, this connects nicely to what I'll cover in the next section.
Stepping back for a second — what does all of this mean for the team in practice?

glossary.meaning: pause to look at the bigger picture

customSentences.text: I want to flag something here that we haven't fully resolved yet.
Long story short — the system works, but we're documenting it properly for the first time.
We're fairly confident in this direction, though we'll keep validating it as we go.
I wouldn't claim this is the only valid approach — but it's the one we'd defend.

glossary.meaning: I'm not arguing / I'm not asserting

customSentences.text: Reasonable people could disagree here, and that's fine.
My gut tells me this is the right call, but I want to back it up with data over time.
Take this with a slight grain of salt — the data set is still small.

glossary.word: take with a grain of salt

customSentences.text: I'd rather flag uncertainty now than pretend everything's nailed down.

We deliberately limited ourselves to two because if everything's a priority, nothing is. Security and Performance Efficiency are the attributes that most directly shape architectural decisions for our current phase — the others stay on the radar, but they don't drive design.

glossary.word: if everything's a priority, nothing is
glossary.meaning: having too many top priorities is the same as having none

customSentences.text: Honestly, it came down to a mix of factors. Server-side rendering matters for SEO on a travel site, the team had more hands-on React experience, and the ecosystem fits where we're going. Angular felt like overkill, and Vue lost out on the team familiarity side.

For a team of our size, microservices would have been overkill. We'd have spent more time on infrastructure than on features. We've kept the monolith but drawn clear internal boundaries, so we can split things out later if scale demands it.

Yes, we use DDD selectively — particularly around booking and payment, which are the parts where the domain complexity is highest. We didn't apply it across the board because forcing DDD on simpler areas would have added more friction than value.

Fair point — availability is definitely on our radar, and I'd be the first to admit it's likely to bite us eventually. For now, the budget constraints mean we accept a degree of downtime risk, but we've documented this as a known sensitivity point.

glossary.word: I'd be the first to admit

customSentences.text: Payment is modelled as a state machine with an explicit refund flow built in. When a booking moves to cancelled, the payment object transitions through its reimbursement states and the customer gets refunded automatically, with the operator notified.

glossary.meaning: include as a core part of

customSentences.text: Self-hosting would have meant carrying the operational burden of backups, patching, and failover ourselves. For a small team, the managed option lets us sleep at night and focus on features. It costs more in dollars, but less in stress.

We've kept costs deliberately low by sticking to managed services where the price-to-pain ratio is good, and self-managing only where we get real leverage. Cloud spend is a few hundred dollars a month at our current scale.

glossary.meaning: cost weighed against the trouble saved

customSentences.text: Right now, Perahuku is fairly loosely coupled — it feeds availability data into the booking platform asynchronously. We've made a deliberate choice to treat it as a system under study rather than baking it deeply into the main architecture.

Good question — we've flagged both as constraints rather than dependencies we control. If one goes down, the affected channel goes down with it. The architecture is structured so we could swap in an alternative provider relatively painlessly, but there'd still be a window of disruption.

Honestly, peak season is something we're going to have to address head-on next year. Today we lean on the elasticity of the cloud provider, but the database is the bottleneck we'll hit first. We've sketched a caching strategy that should buy us a lot of headroom.

Confidentiality is one of the two top quality attributes for exactly that reason. Personal data is encrypted at rest and in transit, access is role-based, and we never log sensitive fields. Payment data we deliberately keep out of our database entirely — it lives with the payment processor.

glossary.meaning: while stored and while being sent

customSentences.text: I'll be honest — DR is still a work in progress. We have automated backups with point-in-time recovery, but we haven't done a full restore drill yet. That's on the roadmap for this quarter, and I'd put it at the top of the residual risk list.

We worked through them with the operators and a small group of regular customers. The scenarios come straight from real pain points they raised — we didn't invent them in a vacuum. That's also why we used the Bass et al. template, to keep them concrete and measurable.

Probably keeping Perahuku as a separate system under study rather than merging it into the main platform. It would have been cleaner architecturally to bring them together, but Perahuku is still finding its audience, and locking it in too early would have been a mistake.

We did consider that early on, especially for the booking side. But the payment integration, the regulatory side, and the operator workflows all push beyond what no-code platforms handle gracefully. We'd have hit the limits within a few months.

glossary.meaning: reach the point where something stops working

customSentences.text: We track the scenarios themselves — each one has a measurable target. Beyond that, we look at lead time for changes, incident rate, and how often the team has to argue about how things should be done. If those metrics trend the right way, the architecture is doing its job.

Honestly, we don't have a formal one yet, and I'd rather be upfront about that than pretend otherwise. What we do have is documentation of where the debt lives, and a habit of paying small amounts down as we touch each area. The architecture work itself is a big down-payment.

We're English-and-Indonesian for now, which covers our main markets. The architecture supports adding languages, but we haven't built it out beyond those two. Currency is handled separately — we always price in IDR and convert at checkout.

Arc42 felt like the right balance of structure and flexibility for our context. C4 is excellent for visualising structure, but arc42 covers decisions, trade-offs, risks, and quality scenarios in one place. For a transferarbeit, that breadth mattered more than diagram polish.

We use optimistic locking on the availability records. If two requests collide, the second one fails and the customer is gently told to pick another slot. It's rare in practice because the time windows are narrow, but the safety net is there.
We're a small team — two engineers and a designer, with operations support shared across the business. That team size has shaped pretty much every architectural call we've made.

The initial system was essentially a prototype that grew. We didn't start with formal prototyping, which is honestly part of why we're doing this architecture work now — to give a system that grew organically the deliberate shape it never had.

We rely on eventual consistency. Availability updates from Perahuku flow into the booking system asynchronously, and we accept a short lag window. For anything safety-critical — payments, refunds — we use synchronous calls with idempotency keys.

Briefly. The cold-start latency was a concern for the search and booking paths, and the cost model gets unpredictable above a certain volume. We've kept it as a possible direction for specific workloads, but not as the default.

Search is one of our performance-critical paths. Right now it's database-backed with the right indexes, which gets us by at current scale. If volume grows, the plan is to introduce a search index — but we haven't pulled that trigger yet.

For European customers, yes — that was a non-negotiable from the start. We have a data processing record, right-to-erasure flows are in place, and personal data retention is bounded. The architecture had to support this from day one.

The elephant in the room is probably that we have a single point of failure at the database layer. Multi-region replication is on the roadmap, but it's not implemented today, and a full outage at our cloud provider would take us down with it.

glossary.meaning: an obvious major issue everyone is avoiding
one component whose failure breaks everything

customSentences.text: Today, onboarding is largely a manual process — we sit down with each operator, walk them through Perahuku, and help them set up their first tours. It doesn't scale, but at our current operator count it lets us catch quality issues early.

glossary.meaning: won't work as the system grows

customSentences.text: Realistically, we'd like to see Kapalsantai become the go-to platform for boat tourism in eastern Indonesia, with operators in two or three new regions and a much more automated booking flow. The architecture decisions we're making now are aimed at making that growth feasible.
